clue: You're starting at the corner of 7th and Broadway — where Route 66 used to end. Look at the street signs at this intersection. What two streets are you standing at?
historicalFact: From 1926 to 1936, 7th & Broadway was the official western terminus of US Route 66. Dust Bowl migrants, dreamers, and drifters who drove 2,448 miles from Chicago ended their journey at this intersection — surrounded by movie palaces, department stores, and 504,000 pedestrians a day.

clue: Look up at 615 S Broadway. A vertical blade sign spells out a city name in large letters on the facade. What does it say?
historicalFact: The Los Angeles Theatre opened January 30, 1931 — the last and most extravagant of 12 movie palaces built on Broadway between 1911 and 1931. Charlie Chaplin helped finance its completion so it could open in time to premiere his film 'City Lights.' His guests on opening night: Albert Einstein and his wife Elsa.

clue: At 648 S Broadway, a sign spells out a name above a multi-story facade. The business behind that sign fed 10,000 people for free during the Great Depression. What name is on the sign?
historicalFact: Clifford Clinton opened Clifton's Cafeteria in 1931 as 'a poor man's nightclub.' His business policy, printed on every check: 'Our cashier will cheerfully accept whatever you wish to pay — or you may dine free.' During a 90-day period, 10,000 people ate without paying. Clinton later helped bust an LAPD corruption ring. Someone planted a bomb in his basement as thanks.

clue: Walk to 636 S Broadway — an ornate theater facade with the word PALACE on its marquee. This is the oldest surviving Orpheum theater in the United States, opened in 1911. Harry Houdini, Will Rogers, and Fred Astaire all performed on its stage. Look at the marquee. What single word is displayed in the largest letters?
verificationNeeded: Confirm PALACE marquee text is visible and legible from street level at 636 S Broadway.
historicalFact: The Palace Theatre opened in 1911 as part of the Orpheum vaudeville circuit — Harry Houdini performed escape acts on this stage. When sound killed vaudeville, it became the Broadway Palace silent movie house in 1926, then a Fox West Coast Theatre in 1929. A million-dollar restoration in 2010 brought it back as a live venue. The oldest of Broadway's 12 palaces, and the only one that survived as a performance space.

clue: Clifton's Cafeteria at 648 S Broadway once ran a second location — Clifton's Silver Spoon — in a jewelry emporium building at 515 W 7th St. That building is your final destination tonight. The Silver Spoon's decorating style left something behind in the new tenant's space. Walk into the bar later and look for mounted animals on the walls. What kind of decorations are they?
historicalFact: Clifton's Silver Spoon Cafeteria occupied the Brock & Company building at 515 W 7th from 1974 until Cedd Moses opened Seven Grand there in 2007. The taxidermy throughout Seven Grand descends from the Clifton's aesthetic — a hunting-lodge-meets-cafeteria style that became the whiskey bar's signature.

clue: You've walked from Broadway to Olive Street. Find the building at 617 S Olive. Look at the name on the facade or entrance. What name is on the building?
historicalFact: The James Oviatt Building at 617 S Olive was built in 1928 by LA's most fashionable haberdasher. He visited the 1925 Paris Exposition, saw Rene Lalique's glass pavilion, and commissioned the artisan's first commercial installation: 30 tons of crystal glass shipped from France. The front doors alone are the largest panels of lead crystal Lalique ever produced.

clue: The figures on the Oviatt Building doors aren't people — they have wings. What are they?
verificationNeeded: Confirm Lalique door figures are identifiable as angels from street level.
historicalFact: Lalique's angels guard the entrance to what was once LA's most exclusive men's clothing store. James Oviatt dressed the bankers who worked one block east on Spring Street — the 'Wall Street of the West.' He wanted his front door to communicate that crossing his threshold meant entering a different world.

clue: Count the stories on the Oviatt Building at 617 S Olive St. How many floors does it have?
historicalFact: Walker & Eisen designed this 12-story Art Deco tower in 1928. The penthouse featured a Turkish bath, gym, tennis court, practice golf links, a rooftop garden, and a pool with sand imported from France for a private beach. James Oviatt lived up there, above the store, above the city.

clue: Between Broadway and Hill Street, south of 7th, a narrow brick-paved alley hides behind modern storefronts. It looks more like Florence than Los Angeles. Find the alley. What is its name?
verificationNeeded: Confirm St. Vincent Court signage is visible at alley entrance(s).
historicalFact: This alley was named for St. Vincent's College — the first institution of higher learning in Southern California, founded by Vincentian Fathers in 1865. The college became Loyola University. The alley became a delivery corridor for Bullock's department store, then an Italian restaurant district in 1957, then the world's largest jewelry collective, and now a row of kebab shops. Five lives in one alley.

clue: I lent money to immigrants that every other bank turned away. I built the largest bank in the world from those bets. My LA headquarters — 12 stories of Neoclassical columns — stands at the corner of 7th and Olive, right next to your final destination. Find the building. What decorative objects surround the bronze front doors?
verificationNeeded: Confirm terra cotta coin sculptures surrounding the bronze doors of the Bank of Italy Building (now Hotel Per La) at 649 S Olive are visible from street level.
historicalFact: Amadeo Giannini founded the Bank of Italy in 1904, lending to Italian immigrants, fishermen, and small farmers that other banks wouldn't touch. He renamed it Bank of America in 1930. The terra cotta coins framing his LA headquarters' doors weren't decorative — they were a statement. Money belongs to everyone.

clue: You've arrived at your destination — a whiskey bar at 515 W 7th St. Before you go in, find the bar's sign or entrance. What is the name of the bar?
historicalFact: George A. Brock opened his jewelry emporium at 515 W 7th Street in 1922 — LA's most important jewelry and giftware store. When Brock's closed, Clifton's Silver Spoon Cafeteria moved in. When that closed, Cedd Moses signed a lease for $1,600 a month for 25,000 square feet and opened Seven Grand. The vintage Brock & Co. display cases that once held diamonds now showcase 900+ whiskeys.

clue: Look at the building at 515 W 7th St. Count the floors from street level to the roofline. How many stories is the Brock & Company Building?
historicalFact: Dodd and Richards designed this four-story Spanish Colonial Revival building in 1922 for George A. Brock's jewelry empire. Brock signed a 99-year lease on the property. He didn't make it — the store closed decades before the lease expired. The building outlasted its purpose three times over.

clue: Look across 7th Street from Seven Grand. A 12-story building at 530 W 7th has a distinctive metal trim running along its roofline — a different color than the surrounding stonework. It was the only one of its kind in LA when built. What metal is it?
verificationNeeded: Confirm copper cornice is visible on the Brockman Building at 530 W 7th from street level.
historicalFact: John Brockman built this 12-story tower in 1912 — the first building west of Broadway to reach the city's 150-foot height limit. His copper cornice was unique in LA. More importantly, his gamble on 7th Street started the westward expansion that brought Brock, Bullock's, Oviatt, and eventually Seven Grand to this corridor.

clue: I opened the finest jewelry store in Los Angeles in 1922. I signed a 99-year lease on my building at 515 W 7th. My display cases were built to hold diamonds, sapphires, and gold watches. I've been gone for decades, but my cases are still here — holding something amber now. Walk inside. What are the vintage display cases holding today?
historicalFact: George A. Brock founded Brock & Company in 1903. By 1922, he'd built LA's most important jewelry emporium at 515 W 7th — a four-story Spanish Colonial Revival showcase. When Cedd Moses opened Seven Grand in 2007, he kept the original Brock & Co. display cases and filled them with 900+ whiskeys. Diamonds became bourbon. The cases didn't care.

clue: The Brockman Building (530 W 7th, across the street) was built in 1912 by John Brockman. The Brock Building (515 W 7th, where you're standing) was built in 1922 by George Brock. Two different men. Same street. Similar names. One arrived first and pulled the other westward. Look at both buildings. The older one across the street is taller. How many more stories does the Brockman Building have than the Brock Building?
historicalFact: Brockman: 12 stories (1912). Brock: 4 stories (1922). Difference: 8. John Brockman's gamble on 7th Street — the first building west of Broadway to hit the 150-foot height limit — created the gravitational pull that drew George Brock, Bullock's department store, and the entire retail district westward. Two unrelated men with near-identical names, building the same corridor a decade apart.
